name: Expert Report on Class Damages
summary: Drafts a comprehensive expert report on class damages for class action litigation, ensuring compliance with Daubert standards and Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 23(b)(3). Provides defensible economic analysis of aggregate class-wide damages to support class certification and damages phases. Use when preparing expert testimony on measurable economic harm in federal or state class actions.
content:
# Expert Report on Class Damages - Enhanced Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Expert Report on Class Damages for litigation purposes. This report must meet the rigorous standards expected in federal and state courts for class action proceedings, providing a defensible economic analysis of damages across an entire class of plaintiffs.

## Document Purpose and Context

Begin by understanding that this expert report serves as critical evidence in class certification and damages phases of class action litigation. The report must withstand Daubert challenges and satisfy Federal Rule of Evidence 702 requirements for expert testimony. Your analysis should demonstrate that damages can be measured on a class-wide basis using a common methodology, which is essential for class certification under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 23(b)(3).

Before drafting, thoroughly review all uploaded case documents to extract key facts including the nature of the alleged harm, the class definition, relevant time periods, and any opposing expert reports. Search for case-specific data such as transaction records, financial statements, surveys, or other empirical evidence that will form the foundation of your damages analysis. Identify the specific legal theories of liability and how they translate into measurable economic harm.

## Title Page and Preliminary Matters

The title page should reflect the formality and precision expected in federal litigation. Include the complete case caption with court name, case number, and all parties. Clearly identify the expert by full name and professional credentials, the date of the report, and the party on whose behalf the expert is retained. Research current formatting standards from federal court local rules and expert witness practice guides to ensure compliance with jurisdictional requirements.

## Introduction and Engagement Overview

Draft an introduction that establishes the expert's role and the scope of opinions to be offered. Explain the class action context concisely, identifying the putative class, the claims asserted, and the relief sought. Frame the key questions the report addresses, such as whether damages can be calculated on a class-wide basis, what the aggregate damages amount to, and whether individual issues predominate over common questions.

Detail the scope of your engagement by describing precisely what tasks were assigned, what materials were reviewed, and what limitations or constraints apply to your analysis. Cross-reference the engagement letter or retention agreement if available in the uploaded documents. Be explicit about what you were not asked to do—for example, you may not be opining on liability, causation, or individual damages allocation. This section protects against scope creep during deposition and trial testimony.

## Expert Qualifications and Credibility

Present the expert's qualifications in a manner that establishes both technical competence and litigation experience. Include educational background with degrees, institutions, and years; professional experience with emphasis on relevant industries or economic sectors; publications and presentations on related topics; and prior testimony history including the number of times testified, courts, and general subject matters. Research best practices for presenting expert credentials in class action damages reports, noting that courts scrutinize whether the expert's background specifically qualifies them for the particular damages methodology employed. Avoid generic credentials—tie qualifications directly to the analytical approach used in this case.

## Methodology and Economic Framework

This section forms the analytical heart of the report. Explain the economic or statistical methodology employed to calculate class-wide damages with sufficient detail that another qualified expert could replicate your analysis. Common methodologies in class damages cases include regression analysis to isolate the effect of the defendant's conduct, before-and-after comparisons using control groups, yardstick approaches comparing affected markets to unaffected markets, or lost profits models based on but-for scenarios.

Justify why the chosen methodology is appropriate for this particular case, citing academic literature, prior court acceptance of similar methods, and alignment with the legal theory of harm. Search for published economic research, Federal Judicial Center reference manuals on expert evidence, and case law discussing the admissibility of similar methodologies. Address potential criticisms proactively, explaining why alternative approaches were rejected or would be less reliable. Ensure your methodology demonstrates that damages can be proven through common evidence rather than requiring individualized inquiries for each class member.

## Data Sources, Assumptions, and Reliability

Identify every data source relied upon in your analysis with specificity. For each source, describe its origin, how it was obtained, any processing or cleaning performed, and why it is reliable for the purposes of damages calculation. Extract relevant data from uploaded financial records, transaction databases, surveys, or other empirical materials. If you relied on data produced by the defendant, note any limitations or concerns about completeness.

Articulate all material assumptions underlying your analysis. These might include assumptions about market conditions, consumer behavior, pricing dynamics, or counterfactual scenarios. Explain why each assumption is reasonable and supported by evidence or accepted economic principles. Research industry standards and economic literature to support key assumptions. Address how sensitive your damages calculations are to changes in assumptions—courts favor analyses that remain robust across reasonable assumption variations.

## Damages Analysis and Calculations

Present your damages calculations in a clear, step-by-step manner that allows the reader to follow your reasoning from raw data to final conclusions. Begin with the aggregate class-wide damages figure, then break down the calculation into its component parts. Show how you determined the number of class members affected, the per-member average harm, and any variations across subgroups within the class.

Use tables, charts, and graphs to illustrate key findings and make complex calculations accessible to judges and juries. For each calculation step, provide the formula used, the data inputs, and the resulting output. If you performed sensitivity analyses or calculated damages under alternative scenarios, present these as ranges with explanations of what drives the variation. Search for examples of damages presentations in similar class action cases to identify effective visualization techniques and organizational structures.

Ensure your calculations account for all elements of damages claimed in the complaint, whether compensatory, restitutionary, or disgorgement-based. If the legal theory supports multiple measures of damages, calculate each separately and explain any overlaps to avoid double-counting. Address issues of mitigation, offsets, or benefits received by class members that might reduce net damages.

## Conclusions and Summary of Opinions

Synthesize your findings into clear, defensible conclusions about the total class-wide damages. State your opinions with appropriate confidence levels, acknowledging any uncertainties or limitations in the available data. If your analysis produces a range rather than a point estimate, explain what factors account for the range and whether any value within it is more probable than others.

Tie your conclusions directly back to the methodology and data discussed in earlier sections, demonstrating the logical chain from evidence to opinion. Research relevant case law on class damages to ensure your conclusions align with legal standards for proving damages with reasonable certainty. Avoid overstating the precision of your estimates—courts are skeptical of damages figures presented with false precision given the inherent uncertainties in economic modeling.

## Appendices and Supporting Materials

Organize all supporting documentation in clearly labeled appendices. These should include detailed calculation spreadsheets, data tables, copies of key source documents, the expert's curriculum vitae, a list of all materials considered, and any demonstrative exhibits you intend to use at trial or in deposition. Research legal templates and expert report exemplars to ensure your appendices follow professional standards and facilitate easy reference during testimony.

Include a complete bibliography of all academic sources, industry reports, and legal authorities cited in the report. For any proprietary models or software used in your analysis, provide sufficient documentation that the methodology remains transparent and replicable.

## Final Document Assembly

Once you have gathered all necessary information from uploaded case documents, conducted appropriate research on methodological standards and legal requirements, and completed all calculations, create a comprehensive expert report document that integrates all sections into a cohesive, professionally formatted litigation document. The final report should be suitable for filing with the court, service on opposing counsel, and use as the foundation for expert testimony at deposition and trial.
